old_claim.note: Art Conservation Workshops is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: Early cabinet catalogs used printed images and woodcuts to represent collection rooms and specimens, making scientific illustration printing an enabling practice.

source_shape.source_locator: BHL describes Imperato's 1599 Dell'historia Naturale as a 791-page printed work with 119 woodcut illustrations and the first published pictorial representation of a Renaissance cabinet of curiosity.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source ties the anchor cabinet catalog to printed woodcut illustration and pictorial representation.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The old conservation-workshop edge described preservation context, while the source-backed mechanism for catalog readers is printed visual representation of objects and rooms.

ontology_before: Public Museum Catalogs depended on Art Conservation Workshops, implying preservation practice was the collection-catalog mechanism.
ontology_after: Printed Cabinet Catalogs depends on scientific illustration printing as the mechanism for representing specimens and collection rooms.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: public_museum_catalogs now depends on scientific_illustration_printing and no longer directly depends on art_conservation_workshops.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to conservation records rather than printed cabinet catalogs.
A source showed early cabinet catalogs required conservation workshops rather than printed illustration practice.
A separate art-conservation catalog node were introduced for restoration records.
short_reason: Printed illustration is source-backed for the catalog; conservation workshops are not.
